title: Secret Passage
setup: Place a small object (stone, coin, folded note) at the top platform (ladder side) — the hidden thing
Walk the route yourself first so you can spot the crawl hole safely
Stand near the crawl hole on first run — close enough to spot, far enough not to guide

activity_flow.instruction: Introduce the mission at the inside entrance. Something is hidden at the top. The only way in is through here.
activity_flow.prompt: "This is the way in. Where do you think it goes?"

activity_flow.instruction: Child walks inside to inside platform. Let them look around the enclosed space.
activity_flow.prompt: "What can you see? Can you spot the way out?"

activity_flow.instruction: Child finds and approaches the crawl hole. Don't demonstrate the orientation — let them work out how their body needs to go.
activity_flow.prompt: "How will you need to go through? What goes first?"

activity_flow.instruction: Child crawls through to the crawl exit. Stay close but don't pull or steer.
activity_flow.prompt: "Arms pulling, legs pushing. You're nearly through."

activity_flow.instruction: Pause at crawl exit. Child reorients — they're now outside, on the back of the apparatus.
activity_flow.prompt: "Where are you now? Where's the wall?"

activity_flow.instruction: Walk to climbing wall base. Child studies the holds before climbing.
activity_flow.prompt: "What do you notice about the two sides of the wall?"

activity_flow.instruction: Climb grips side to climbing wall mid. Feet pushing, not dangling.
activity_flow.prompt: "Find the next grip. Where does your foot go?"

activity_flow.instruction: At climbing wall mid (grips), child traverses sideways to the holes side. This is a genuine planning moment — lateral shift at height. Stay close.
activity_flow.prompt: "You need to get across without coming down. One hand, then one foot."

activity_flow.instruction: Climb holes side to top platform. Collect the hidden object.
Descend ladder from top platform to ladder mid, then to ladder base.
activity_flow.prompt: "Face the ladder. Come down one rung at a time."

adult_guidance.headline: Don't demo the crawl orientation
adult_guidance.detail: Working out which way to enter the hole — head first or feet first, which side to face — is a spatial reasoning task. Solving it independently is more valuable than getting through quickly.

adult_guidance.headline: The traverse is the curriculum peak
adult_guidance.detail: Shifting weight sideways at height (A2→A1) is the hardest move on this route. Midline crossing, grip adjustment, weight shift — give it time. One cue maximum: 'one hand, then one foot.'

adult_guidance.headline: Reorientation at the crawl exit is real work
adult_guidance.detail: Coming out the other side disoriented is normal and useful. The question 'where are you now?' is spatial language work, not filler. Wait for their answer before moving on.

adult_guidance.headline: Ladder down is a controlled descent
adult_guidance.detail: After the intensity of the wall and traverse, the ladder descent may feel easy — but children often rush. 'One rung at a time, face the ladder' is the only cue needed.

variations: Reverse the wall: climb holes side first (start at ladder base, walk around), traverse to grips, descend grips — same nodes, different direction
Double crawl: go through the hole, come back through from outside before climbing — doubles the orientation challenge
Silent run: adult gives no prompts after step 2 — child navigates entirely independently
free_play: Find every hidden spot on the apparatus
Can you find two ways to get to the top of the wall?
What other routes use the secret passage?
why_it_works: The crawl hole shifts movement from vertical to horizontal, demanding core stability and bilateral limb coordination in a plane that climbing cannot reach. Children must independently solve the spatial problem of how to orient their body — proprioceptive and spatial reasoning work that happens before they move a limb. The wall traverse that follows is the most laterally demanding element on the apparatus: weight shifting sideways at height, arms crossing the midline, grip adjusting continuously. The ladder descent closes the route from the front, giving the child a clear sense of having moved all the way through the apparatus from back to front.
